sentences.text: Very little.
Really small street, and they must have destroyed the synagogue that was on the corner, that I told you, where we went to with my father-- I went to with my father-- when I knocked down the chestnut.
That was in the same block, in the far corner.
But I don't remember.
I know, you know, I must be repressing it, because we must have heard things, and we must have seen something, not much, because it was a tiny window.
But you know, it was a frightening experience, just to be cooped up in that cellar.
Really, it was sort of like a solitary confinement.
I don't remember.
No.
We went down in the morning.
And stayed all day till the evening.
No.
Very small.
Like a solitary confinement cell, I would assume.
No.
No.
We just stood there all day.
Very little, because we didn't want to talk.
I mean, it wasn't a good idea to, to make any sounds.
We couldn't.
I don't think-- we barely talked because-- and I guess he was as-- very frightened.
It was a very frightening experience, to be cooped up there, and not know what went upstairs, what would happen to my mother, what would happen to my grandmother, what would happen to us.
I don't recall.
I assume I would have, yeah.
But I, I don't recall.
There are certain things that you, you just repress and don't remember.
For instance, I should mention-- we might come to that later, but-- the one thing-- and I have a good memory.
I have a good visual memory.
And I remember that cellar very clearly, that cold, cold cellar.
But for instance, I can't remember when I was evacuated, after having been in England for two months.
And again, we were evacuated into the countryside when the British assumed the bombing would start.
And I cannot remember how I got to that town.
By bus, by train, by walking, I have no idea.
And I've been trying to, to-- you know, I've thought about that.
So it must have been a very traumatic experience to-- again, being uprooted, and to again, have to leave the place.
And I can't remember.
So, I also don't remember really what, what-- whether we talked or what we talked about.
I don't remember.
And who was it that brought you and your father out?
Was-- who came to take you out of the coal cellar?
I would think my mother came down, and-- or maybe the janitor.
I don't remember.
I'm not sure.
Oh.
These are the-- anyway.
Shall I continue?
Well, we knew that was the beginning of the end.
[DOOR OPENING] We knew that, because the, the-- all the synagogues had been--
OK.
Mhm.
Well, we were devastated.
As much as the physical devastation, there was that psychological devastation, of realizing what danger we, we were in.
The men had been taken away.
Many of the men had been taken away.
And the synagogues had been destroyed, the jewelry shops had been-- the windows had been broken.
Swastikas had been painted-- or not swastikas, but the word 'Jew' had been painted on all the Jewish stores.
So there was-- the temples were still burning.
So we knew this was it.
And we know what, what would happen if we weren't aware that-- well, we were aware that there was already concentration camps.
And so we really were aware of what was going to happen.
And, and-- and we were beginning to make plans, my parents, about how to leave the country.
I don't remember whether they did.
They must have.
Just as I said, they were in such close proximity that, that, you know, I'm sure we would have passed it.
I don't recall myself.
I don't recall that.
And it's, it's interesting that I don't, because I'm just thinking, was I-- I was still in school at the time, and I should have passed it.
And I guess the building was-- you know, the outside walls were standing.
And-- but I don't recall now.
I really don't.
Again, I'm, I'm sure that there was such a fear that, that permeated our lives at that time, at the moment, that, that you sort of block that out.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, sure.
Sure.
Well, they, they had broken windows, and some were completely destroyed and looted.
And others were just immediately taken over, and some were shut.
And they immediately installed Kommissars, they called them, Kommissars who, who took over the Jewish stores.
So my uncle, who had the jewelry store, his store was taken over.
And all the stores were taken over at that time.
And I think that's why it was such a tremendous shock.
And in some ways, a good thing, because we weren't allowed into complacency or hope that, oh well, things are going to get better, and blah, blah, blah.
But no.
This was it.
We knew what was going on.
I very much felt-- as a child, I was still a youngster-- felt that this was the end of the world as I knew it.
It was very clear to me.
Yeah.
Yes, sure.
I think one of my uncles was taken.
And as I said, one of my father's very close friends.
He used to play cards with him, came to the apartment all the time.
They had a game going.
He was taken.
And many others that we-- that, you know, we knew, or, or acquaintances, or, or people of acquaintances, of people we knew.
So sure, many, many people.
They were eventually released.
The people who were taken in Kristallnacht were not killed, because they-- that was before the war, and the killing started much later.
So they were-- they were there for about three or four months, maybe, and then they were released.
Well, we-- [CLEARS THROAT] --we dug in, dug in our resources.
We knew-- saved every penny we could.

And I am beginning to wonder where-- I don't know when my mother wrote-- went to the-- at that time, I think a couple months later, I started to go to the Zionist organization, youth organization, and started to get involved in that.
Yeah.
It was very soon after I quit school.
And I must have been in May, June, of '38.
And I don't remember when she wrote to England, because it must have-- the process must have taken quite a bit of time.
It might have been that year that she wrote.
And--
To look for a sponsor who would take me to, you know, deposit the money, and-- oh, I talked to you in private before.
They-- England was taking in children who were supposedly-- supposedly had another place to go to, but this was a transitory stop.
And it was called the Kindertransport, the children's transport.
And eventually, England took in about 10,000 children from Germany and Austria.
And so my mother was trying to find every possible way.
We, we had-- my father had three brothers in the United States.
And so we wrote to them, and they sent us the affidavits which would enable us to come to the United States.
And there were quota numbers.
Each country had a quota number.
It depended on the births-- of your place of birth, of what your quota number would be.
And Austria had a very large number, quota number, quota numbers, which allowed people to come to the United States if they had an affidavit.
I believe it was 10,000 a year.
I might be mistaken, I'm not sure.
But my father-- so my mother and I were born in, in Austria, so we fell under that quota.
My father was born in Romania, which had a very, very small quota, about 290 people a year, because the Immigration Act that the United States enacted in '23 limited people from Eastern Europe.
And so his chances of, of coming to the United States were nil, because we had a rough idea of what number was given at any one point-- and not exactly, but fairly close.
So we knew that there would be no hope for him.
We knew that for us, there was a good possibility that we might make it, because we knew that war would start in the fall of '39.
It was well known.
Hitler didn't make it a secret.
And we were pretty sure that's when-- that was-- started wars in the fall, because it was a favorable time of year to start, to have the tanks, start the tanks to move.
So we were pretty sure war would start then.
And-- but even if it hadn't, just the idea of, of living under, under the Nazis was enough to make you leave as quickly as possible. so we explored every, every possibility.
When I say we, it was my parents who did it.
And Shanghai took in people.
Cuba took in people.
But these places opened up all of a sudden and then closed down again very quickly.
So if you weren't there at the right moment, or didn't whatever, didn't have enough money, in some way deposit an awful lot of money to get there, you, you didn't make it.
And so that didn't work.
So my mother wanted to-- and my parents wanted me to be safe.
And so my mother wrote to-- my mother, who spoke English fairly well and wrote English well, better then even than she spoke, went to the-- did what a lot of people had done.
Go to the telephone company and get-- that's where you-- who had the telephone books of different nations.
And people would-- who would want to come to the United States would-- we knew people who had written hundreds and hundreds of letters appealing to strangers to send them an affidavit.
So-- but she only got 10, 10 telephone numbers in England.
And she got the numbers of garment manufacturers, assuming that they were Jewish, and two answered.
A Jewish man in Manchester answered.
And you had to-- they had to deposit 200 pounds, which was the equivalent of $1,000 in those days.
And they had to guarantee the stay of the child.
And he said he would be able to take me in, but he could not deposit the money.
And a Mr.
George of Bradford, England, said, yes.
He would be able to put the money-- which was supposed to have been repaid when I left, because what the money was meant for, passage, the final passage to-- passage to another country.
And he would take me.
The money must have gone to a Jewish organization.
There was a Jewish organization who took care of that whole project in England.
And so he deposited the money, and-- which wasn't-- as I think now, and I've thought for many years, was a totally amazing thing for a stranger to, to respond like that.
And for him-- and I knew it afterwards, it was very simple.
Like so many of those people who had helped the Jews, that somebody appealed to you for help, and you had to respond.
There was no-- this was not a question of-- he didn't agonize over that.
He did that.
And he did it on his own.
I don't think his wife really supported him 100%, but he did it.
He was, he was a wonderful man.
He came after the war.
He came to the United States, to visit us.
Just to see how we were doing.
And I wanted to repay him at that time, and he wouldn't take the money.
His-- one of his daughters is still alive.
She is 85, and I still call her and I'm in touch with her.
So that's how I eventually got to England.
No.
That was not an affidavit.
That was-- this-- that was done even in Vienna through the Kindertransport organization.
There was an organization in Vienna which worked with the, the British government, and the British organization in England.
And so we had nothing to do with it.
That was handled by that organization.
They took care of it.
Because the, the transport-- I was on an actual transport train-- and there were many children.
I forgot how many.
Maybe there could have been 200, 300.
We had our own cars of that train.
That-- so there might have been a couple of hundred children on that train.
And most of them went into camps in England that were set up for children.
Some went to-- [COUGHS] Excuse me.
Some of the children went to farms.
Farmers took them in.
They did agricultural work on farms.
But a lot of them stayed in camps.
I did not.
I actually stayed-- I did not stay with Mr. , but I stayed with his designer and his wife, who were the young couple.
Their name was [?
Candler, ?] and I stayed with them while I was-- for not all the time, but till I-- till I was evacuated.
And then when I came back again.
Mhm.
Well, the question was, you know, this was-- now-- now we are moving into, into 1939.
And as I said, war was imminent.
Now, now it was getting really tight.
And the question was, would the affidavit come through?
And my father, at that time, realized his only hope would be to go on an illegal ship to Palestine.
He could not go-- get out any other way.
And my mother and I still had held hope for getting that visa to the United States.
But again, it was getting very close.
And so when the-- since by July-- and it was a question, which would come first.
And then I knew that by July, I could go on that-- July 4th, I was supposed to go on that train to England.
So if I didn't get my affidavit-- my visa before-- United States visa-- then I would go on that train to England.
And I think it was a very courageous thing of them to send me to, you know, to safety, and not wait till I would go with my mother to the United States.
Luckily, she was able to, and the last ships were [INAUDIBLE] after the war started, she and her brother got out and came to the United States.
Yeah.
Well, I had that steamer trunk my father had made.
We took a taxi.
I remember it very vividly.
My father talked to me the day before.
He took me for a walk.
He gave me a sex education lecture, warned me about all the evils of, of prostitutes and what have you.
And I-- while I listened-- and I still remember the walk.
We were walking on the Danube canal.
And I listened, and you know, my heart was pretty heavy at that point, because I knew, I knew the pain he must have experienced, and my own pain.
And I was beginning to, you know, really feel very difficult.
And then the next day, or a couple days later, we went.
And they took me to the train station.
And--
Well, they just told me to-- you know, to write, and to let them know how everything went on.
But it was heavy.
It was pretty heavy.
Nobody said too much at that point.
And we all felt-- we all knew how we felt, and what it meant.
And, you know-- and I remember myself standing at the, at the window in the train station, and you know, looking at them.
They were standing below.
And wondering, you know, whether I would ever see them again.
And yet, there was some feeling on my part that, that I was glad that I was getting out.
You know, there was that feeling too.
I was 15.
Well, whatever-- clothes.
They bought a lot of new clothes.
And I remember a couple of heavy flannel nightshirts, long nightshirts, that my-- maybe my mother made it, even.
I don't remember.
She was very good at sewing things.
She had a pattern shop as a young woman, among other things.
And they had bought me a lot of clothes.
And I don't even know whether I got any of them, I guess there might have been a few dollars that I had, but that was about it.
Just filled with clothes and stuff.
So they, they put you on the train, and did you know where you were going at that time?
Yeah.
I knew I was going to England.
Sure.
Well, the train went through Austria and through Germany.
And-- well, which was-- actually there were no more borders, so it was called Austria.
As a matter of fact, when I looked at the, my mother's passport, I realized that it said on it 'Deutsches Reich', and I realized.
You know, I never thought about it really, but of course, we were part of Germany.
And then the train went into Holland.
And on the border, I remember-- the one thing I remember-- well, two things.
One was I, I, I was sitting next to-- by coincidence, I was sitting next to the son of that relative of my uncle.
And maybe my uncle arranged it, I don't remember.
Because he was active in that organization, who had been-- whose father had been killed, that I mentioned to you, who had-- where the Nazis threw the bomb.
One of the first Nazi victims in Austria.
And Peter Weiss, who eventually became Peter White, and is one of the editors of-- retired now-- In the United States.
So we were sitting together.
I didn't-- I might have met him once or twice before, but I remember we talked a lot.
And then at the border, the SS came in and sort of looked us over.
They didn't really do much, but they looked around.
And they left the train, and then we were in Holland.
And we were-- Was the train all a Kindertransport or--
No.
No.
So it was just some cars.
There were a couple of cars.
Yeah, maybe three cars, maybe.
Yeah.
And then we went to Hoek van Holland, which is on a hook, actually, on the coast, which is why it's called Hoek van Holland.
And there we went on to a ship at night.
Trip took-- from, from Vienna to Hoek van Holland took-- we left at about 4:00 in the afternoon, maybe, 4:00 or 5:00, and got there about four o'clock.
So it took 24 hours to get-- to get there.
Yeah.
No.
I don't know who fed us, or what we ate.
I have no recollection of that.
I don't remember that.
No.
And all I remember then is getting onto the ship, and we had a cabin.
I don't know whether I shared it with anybody.
I'm assuming I did.
And then in the morning we were-- it didn't take that long, but the-- we slept.
And in the morning, we woke up in probably Dover, England.
And then we got off the ship.
And then they-- and they are representatives of the Jewish agency, waited, waited for us.
And they put us on a train to London.
I don't remember that.
There must have been.
Yeah.
No.
No.
Well, There might have been.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS] But I, I don't remember that.
I think there must have been, must have been.
Thinking back, I was very anxious at that point, of, you know, not knowing the-- barely knowing the language.
I had taken some English lessons.
Another cousin of mine was fluent, and she was a professor in English.
And she was fluent, and she gave me lessons for a little bit.
So I knew a little bit, few words.
Very few.
And then in London, since we went to different places-- children went to all different places in England-- there was a, a representative, again, of the, the Jewish agency in London.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There were people there.
And they put us on a train to London, or maybe went with us to London.
I don't remember.
They must have.
They must have.
And then I don't remember whether it was one of those people who were on the train, or whether somebody new came in, in London.
But I do remember a woman speaking to me, and taking me in tow, and speaking Yiddish to me, because my English wasn't-- was barely there.
And she didn't speak German, but she did speak Yiddish, which was close enough.
And putting me on another-- must have been another station, unless we got in the same station.
I don't remember.
And putting me on the train.
Whether she gave me a name tag or not I don't know.
I don't know whether the-- how the steamer trunk-- whether it followed me or what.
Did eventually wind up in Bradford.
And I got on the train.
It must have been about a five hour trip.
And so actually, it took two days before I got from, got from Vienna to Bradford.
And on the station, Mr.
Hurst was there, and welcomed me.
And, and his designer, I think, was there too.
And he spoke Yiddish, so that helped, till I started-- and I went to school.
